content.text: How can I implement a standard VGG-style convolutional block using TensorFlow Probability layers?

To create this network block, we will stack two main sets of convolutions. Each convolution is followed by Batch Normalization and ReLU activation to capture spatial features.

```python
def _vggconv_block(x, filters, kernel, stride, kernel_posterior_fn):
  """Network block for VGG."""
  out = tfp.layers.Convolution2DFlipout(
      filters,
      kernel,
      padding='same',
      kernel_posterior_fn=kernel_posterior_fn)(x)
  out = tf.keras.layers.BatchNormalization()(out)
  out = tf.keras.layers.Activation('relu')(out)

  out = tfp.layers.Convolution2DFlipout(
      filters,
      kernel,
      padding='same',
      kernel_posterior_fn=kernel_posterior_fn)(out)
  out = tf.keras.layers.BatchNormalization()(out)
  out = tf.keras.layers.Activation('relu')(out)

  out = tf.keras.layers.MaxPooling2D(
      pool_size=(2, 2), strides=stride)(out)
  return out
```

This function successfully implements the core building blocks of a VGG network using probabilistic convolutional layers.
What happens if I set the `stride` parameter in the MaxPooling layer to 1?
If the stride is 1, the spatial dimensions (height and width) will not change after the pooling step. The purpose of pooling is typically dimensionality reduction.
That clarifies how the block transforms the input data.
